title: Temple of Hibis
description: The finest example of Persian period architecture in Egypt, dedicated to the Theban triad (Amun, Mut, and Khonsu). It is the largest and best-preserved temple in the Western Desert.

title: Bagawat Necropolis
description: One of the oldest and best-preserved Christian cemeteries in the world, featuring hundreds of mudbrick chapels, some with vivid biblical murals like the Chapel of the Exodus and Chapel of Peace.

title: Temple of Nadura
description: A temple fortress situated on a strategic hill offering panoramic views of the oasis. Dedicated to Amun and Mut, built during the reign of Emperor Antoninus Pius.

title: Temple of Ghweita (Qasr Al-Ghueita)
description: A fortified temple settlement atop a hill, dedicated to the Theban Triad. The current structure dates largely to Darius I and the Ptolemies.

title: Temple of Qasr Al-Zayan
description: A sandstone temple dedicated to Amenebis (Amun of Hibis), located within a mudbrick fortress in the village of Qasr Al-Zayan.

title: Temple of Dush (Kysis)
description: Located in the ancient border town of Kysis. Features a sandstone temple dedicated to Isis and Serapis, adjacent to a massive mudbrick fortress that guarded caravan routes.

title: Qasr Al-Labkha
description: A spectacular remote site featuring two temples, a rock-cut shrine, and a massive fortress (The White House) connected to spring sources by intricate ancient aqueducts.

title: Umm El-Dabadib
description: An imposing remote fortress with high towers, accompanied by a settlement and an extensive, sophisticated underground aqueduct system (qanats) descending from the cliffs.

title: Ain Manawir
description: An ancient settlement excavated recently, revealing a unique system of qanats (water tunnels) irrigating fields and a mudbrick temple dedicated to Osiris-Io.

title: Gebel Al-Teir
description: The 'Mountain of Birds', known for its rock inscriptions ranging from Prehistoric times to Coptic graffiti written by hermits, including prayers to the Virgin Mary.

title: Deir El-Hagar
description: The 'Monastery of Stone', a well-preserved sandstone temple on the western edge of Dakhla dedicated to the Theban Triad and Thoth, featuring clear astronomical ceilings.

title: Al-Qasr Islamic Village
description: A magnificently preserved medieval mudbrick town built over a Roman site. Features covered streets, cool courtyards, an Ayyubid mosque, and carved wooden lintels with Quranic inscriptions.

title: Qaret Al-Muzawaka
description: The 'Decorated Hill', a necropolis featuring rock-cut tombs, most notably the colorful tombs of Petosiris and Sadosiris which blend Egyptian pharaonic and Roman art styles.

title: Bashendi Village
description: A picturesque village built over an ancient site. Contains the Roman sandstone tomb of Kitines and the Islamic domed mausoleum of Sheikh Bashendi.

title: Balat (Qila' El-Dabba)
description: The necropolis of the Governors of the Oasis. Features large mudbrick mastabas, including those of Khentika and Medunefer, marking Dakhla's importance in early history.

title: Ain Asil
description: The ancient administrative settlement associated with the Balat necropolis. Provides rare evidence of Old Kingdom urban life and administration in the desert.

title: Ismant El-Kharab (Kellis)
description: The ruins of ancient Kellis, a major town deserted in the 4th century. Features the Temple of Tutu, early Christian churches, and domestic architecture.

title: Amheida (Trimithis)
description: A buried city featuring a temple of Thoth and the House of Serenos, which contained elaborate mythological wall paintings now displayed in the Visitors Center.

title: Teneida
description: Site of ancient rock carvings and hieroglyphic inscriptions, historically a key water stop on caravan routes coming from the Nile Valley.

title: Mut Al-Kharab
description: The ancient capital of Dakhla (Mothis), site of the Temple of Seth. Archaeological works have yielded ostraca and temple remains within the modern city walls.

title: Bir Al-Shaghala
description: A necropolis recently discovered near Mut containing multi-level tombs with well-preserved colorful paintings and mummies.

title: Ain El-Sabil
description: Features a small temple dedicated to Amun and remains of a settlement, located near Ismant el-Kharab.

title: Deir El-Munira
description: A large Roman mudbrick fortress located on the road between Kharga and Asyut, likely serving as a tax collection point and defensive outpost.

title: Ain Amur
description: A remote temple and settlement situated on the ancient darb (track) connecting the two oases, dedicated to Amun, providing a vital rest stop.

title: Qasr El-Sumaira
description: A square Roman fortress located north of Qasr Al-Labkha, part of the defensive network guarding the oasis approaches.

title: Qasr El-Geb
description: A mudbrick fortress located near Qasr El-Sumaira, positioned to control desert routes.

title: Qasr Farafra
description: The ruins of the ancient Roman fortress which served as the foundation for the traditional clay village of Farafra (largely modernized now).

title: Ain Dallaf
description: Located north of Qasr Farafra, this area contains ruins of a Roman agricultural settlement and ancient springs.

title: Wadi Hennis
description: Contains ruins of a Roman village near the White Desert, showing evidence of ancient cultivation.

title: Cave of Swimmers
description: Famous rock art site featuring depictions of people swimming, suggesting a much wetter climate in the past. Popularized by the film 'The English Patient'.

title: Cave of Beasts (Foggini Cave)
description: A massive rock shelter discovered in 2002, containing thousands of well-preserved paintings, including mysterious headless beasts and negative handprints.

title: Abu Ballas
description: 'Hill of Pottery'. An ancient station on a desert track discovered with hundreds of large pottery jars used as water depots for caravans.

title: Maghetta
description: Site of rock inscriptions and Roman water management systems.

title: Deir Abu Metta
description: Ruins of a Coptic church basilica and monastery buried in the sand dunes near Al-Qasr.
